records.ety: [F. enjoindre, L. injungere to join into, charge, enjoin; in + jungere to join. See Join, and cf. Injunction.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Enjoined (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Enjoining.]

defs: To lay upon, as an order or command; to give an injunction to; to direct with authority; to order; to charge.
quotes: I am enjoined by oath to observe three things.

defs: To prohibit or restrain by a judicial order or decree; to put an injunction on.
quotes: This is a suit to enjoin the defendants from disturbing the plaintiffs.
notes: ☞ Enjoin has the force of pressing admonition with authority; as, a parent enjoins on his children the duty of obedience. But it has also the sense of command; as, the duties enjoined by God in the moral law. “This word is more authoritative than direct, and less imperious than command.”
